paper_id: Allamong - 2024 - Political Alienation and the Trump Vote in the 2016 and 2020 US Presidential Elections

metadata.title: Political Alienation and the Trump Vote in the 2016 and 2020 US Presidential Elections

components.epistemic_function_evidence: Here I briefly explore the content of these responses using a semiautomated text analysis approach-the Structural Topic Model (Roberts et al. 2014)-to identify themes or "topics" related to Trump's "political outsider" qualities, and to examine whether the public's use of these topics varied between 2016 and 2020.
components.downstream_description: A single topic representing Trump's 'political outsider' qualities is identified and interpreted using exemplar quotes. The topic's proportions are then used as a dependent variable in a regression on a survey year indicator to compare its prevalence between the 2016 and 2020 elections, with results also plotted.
components.downstream_evidence: From here I take advantage of the STM's ability to estimate the proportion of each response dedicated to each topic in the model, and regress the "political outsider" topic proportion on a survey year indicator for 2020 (with 2016 as the reference category; see Supplementary Material section 3.3, table S11). I then plot these predicted values for each year in figure 3, where a clear gap emerges: respondents dedicated an average of 6.5 percent of their response to the outsider topic in 2016 (left), but only 2.5 percent in 2020 (right).

downstream.downstream_description: The study uses a Structural Topic Model on open-ended survey responses to explore why the relationship between cynicism and voting for Trump diminished between 2016 and 2020. A single topic related to Trump's 'political outsider' persona is identified and qualitatively interpreted. The proportions of this topic are then used as the dependent variable in a regression against a year indicator to show that this theme was significantly less prevalent in 2020.

paper_id: Alvarez and Morrier - 2024 - Issue Responsiveness in Canadian Politics Are Parties Responsive to the Public Salience of Climate

metadata.title: Issue Responsiveness in Canadian Politics: Are Parties Responsive to the Climate Change's Public Salience in Question Period?

data.data_source_detail: transcripts of each Question Period held in Canada's House of Commons from the 39th legislature, which launched after the January 23, 2006 election, to the 43rd legislature, which culminated in the September 20, 2021 election.
data.preprocessing_details: we first remove all numbers, punctuation marks, and extraneous white spaces. All remaining terms are then lower-cased and stemmed according to Porter's algorithm so that all words are reduced to their root. Finally, to pick out the terms that best allow to distinguish across the various topics, we remove all stop words unlikely to convey any meaningful information and only retain features that occur between one and 25 % of documents.

model.K_evidence: After inspection of diagnostic values for various number of topics, we use the model with 15 topics to carry out the analysis since it offers the best combination of likelihood and semantic coherence.

K_selection.selection_evidence: After inspection of diagnostic values for various number of topics, we use the model with 15 topics to carry out the analysis since it offers the best combination of likelihood and semantic coherence.

components.downstream_description: The prevalence of the 'environment and climate change' topic in political speeches is plotted over time for different political parties and compared descriptively to the public salience of the issue, showing a strong correlation.
components.downstream_evidence: Figure 4 illustrates the evolution over time of the share of parties' speeches devoted to the environment and climate change, which are represented by colored curves, and the public salience of these issues in Canada, which is represented by a thick black curve.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: This result stresses the importance of parties' role in shaping the political agenda and drawing attention to emerging policy concerns such as the climate crisis.
components.downstream_description: The prevalence of the 'environment and climate change' topic is used as the dependent variable in OLS and 2SLS regression models to causally estimate parties' issue responsiveness to public salience, including interactions for different parties and time periods.
components.downstream_evidence: As can be seen in column ( 5), the ratio of a party's speeches related to the environment and climate change increases on average by 0.388 % following a one percent increase in these policy issues' public salience.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: FIGURE 6. Causal Estimates of Feedback and Substitution Effects
components.downstream_description: The prevalences of various other topics are used as dependent variables in a causal model to estimate feedback and substitution effects, i.e., how discussion of other topics changes in response to the public salience of climate change.
components.downstream_evidence: In reaction to an increase of the environment and climate change's public salience, the Liberal Party is found to talk less about infrastructure and natural resources.

downstream.downstream_description: The authors use the prevalence of topics in political speeches, derived from a topic model, to analyze political issue responsiveness. First, they descriptively plot the prevalence of the 'environment and climate change' topic over time for different parties, comparing it to public salience. Second, they use this topic's prevalence as a dependent variable in a 2SLS regression model to make causal claims about how parties' speech content responds to public salience. Third, they extend this causal analysis to other topics to measure substitution effects, i.e., which topics are discussed less when climate change salience increases.

paper_id: Aslett et al. - 2022 - What Was the Problem in Parkland Using Social Media to Measure the Effectiveness of Issue Frames

metadata.title: What Was the Problem in Parkland? Using Social Media to Measure the Effectiveness of Issue Frames

data.data_source_detail: The analysis includes tweets from 24 gun control and 13 gun rights Twitter accounts, as well as three prominent individuals' accounts.

model.algorithm_detail: The stm framework builds from a latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) model. With the addition of the covariates, sparse additive generative (SAGE) topics are enabled automatically in stm.
model.K_evidence: We used the diagnostic tools of the Structural Topic Model (stm) package to compare a large number of models before deciding that a 32 topic mixed membership model best captured our subject of interest (Roberts, Stewart, Tingley, & Airoldi, 2013).

K_selection.selection_evidence: We used the diagnostic tools of the Structural Topic Model (stm) package to compare a large number of models before deciding that a 32 topic mixed membership model best captured our subject of interest (Roberts, Stewart, Tingley, & Airoldi, 2013).

labeling.labeling_evidence: We then used the most common topic terms and some of the tweets that were most strongly associated with a topic to give the topic a label and decide whether the topic highlighted a problem that implied a particular solution (i.e., whether the topic qualified as a problem frame).
labeling.label_validation_evidence: We add another step to address potential concerns about validity-manual validation. We first choose a relatively low probability threshold (0.1) to identify tweets that are potentially about each of the frames/topics. We then manually inspect them, excluding those not judged to be relevant. More specifically, the authors began by labeling a sample of 100 to 200 tweets per frame or topic for relevance (expressed as a binary yes or no for relevant or not). We then used this "gold standard" data set to train six undergraduate coders. When they achieved acceptable reliability (comparing our labels to theirs), we asked them to label all of the other tweets above the 0.1 threshold in each frame and topic. We also continued to monitor their reliability by having two coders label 10 percent of the tweets. ... For the 10 percent of tweets labeled by two coders, there was 90 percent agreement in 10 of the 11 frames/topics, and 85 percent agreement for one other.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: The central advantage of unsupervised topic modeling is that it enables the discovery of common themes within large volumes of text. We did not begin with a predefined set of problem frames, but instead used topic modeling to reveal the frames and topics that were actually being used by gun rights and gun control groups on Twitter.
components.validation_evidence: We then manually validated the individual tweets assigned to each frame and topic with high probability to ensure that the tweets assigned to each frame and topic were actually relevant.
components.downstream_description: Topics are qualitatively interpreted and categorized as 'frames' or 'non-frame topics', and further as 'systemic' or 'individual' frames. The prevalence of these frames and topics is then descriptively compared between gun control and gun rights groups using proportions of total tweets.
components.downstream_evidence: Figure 4b compares proportions of total tweets to provide a better sense of relative frame emphasis. The vast majority of tweets by gun control organizations focused on two systemic frames, "political action" (calling for action among their supporters) and "assault weapons."

components.downstream_description: The identified frames and topics are used as primary independent variables in an OLS regression model to predict the number of retweets a tweet receives. The model includes an interaction between the frame/topic and the type of group (gun rights or gun control) to assess differential effectiveness.
components.downstream_evidence: To better control for such confounders, we estimate an ordinary least squares (OLS) regression model predicting the number of retweets of a tweet. Our primary independent variables are the different tweet frames/topics interacted with the type of group (gun rights or gun control).

downstream.downstream_description: The authors first use topic modeling to identify 'frames' and 'topics' in tweets. They qualitatively interpret these, create a typology (e.g., systemic vs. individual frames), and descriptively compare their usage frequencies between gun control and gun rights groups. Subsequently, they use these identified frames/topics as independent variables in an OLS regression model to predict the number of retweets, thereby testing the effectiveness of different frames for each group.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: These findings support H 3 , showing that Republican women took a hardline stance to establish their conservative bona fides and to signal their willingness to use force in the face of gender stereotypes.
components.downstream_description: A qualitative analysis examines the distinguishing words used by men and women from each party within specific subtopics, using exemplar quotes to illustrate differences in framing and substance.
components.downstream_evidence: We next examine the words that distinguish speeches given by women and men from each party on specific subtopics. Women, however, raised "intelligence" as a means of addressing these problems.

downstream.downstream_description: The study employs a two-stage topic modeling process. First, an initial STM identifies a broad 'Defense and Military' topic, which is used to filter the corpus of speeches. The proportion of each legislator's speeches on this topic is then used as a dependent variable in a regression to compare attention to defense by gender and party. Second, another STM is run on the filtered defense speeches to identify eight subtopics. The proportions of these subtopics are also used as dependent variables in regression models to compare focus within defense policy across groups. Finally, the analysis includes a qualitative deep-dive into how men and women from each party discuss specific subtopics, using distinguishing words and exemplar quotes to highlight differences in framing and priorities.

This is supplemented with qualitative interpretation of exemplar messages. Second, it uses the topic model to filter and select a sample of messages with high topic proportions for subsequent hand-coding to analyze the level of agreement versus disagreement within conversations.

paper_id: Bagozzi - 2015 - The multifaceted nature of global climate change negotiations

metadata.title: The multifaceted nature of global climate change negotiations

data.data_source_detail: Statements given by countries' High-level UNFCCC political representatives during the High-level Segments to the UNFCCC's COPs 16 (2010), 17 (2011), 18 (2012), and 19 (2013).
data.preprocessing_details: The entire corpus was machine-translated to English. For each speech, the text was processed to remove all proper nouns, punctuation, numbers, and stopwords. All remaining words were converted to lower case and stemmed.

model.algorithm_detail: The study estimated LDA models using Gibbs sampling.
model.K_evidence: a total of 25 topics were chosen for the primary LDA model discussed below

K_selection.selection_evidence: Measures of both model perplexity and marginal likelihood were used to determine the ideal number of common topics underlying my collection of 554 climate speeches. Specifically, I estimated LDA models for my entire corpus with the number of topics, k, set to k = {5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 50, 100}, and calculated each model's corresponding posterior log-likelihoods and perplexity measures at each k. ... Figure 1a-b suggest in this case that an optimal number of topics for my corpus rests somewhere in the k = 25 to k = 30 range, since this general range yields the lowest and highest values for my perplexity and log-likelihood measures, respectively. In order to provide the most conservative test of my hypotheses, as well as to guard against potential overfitting, a total of 25 topics were chosen for the primary LDA model discussed below

labeling.labeling_evidence: I present the top 10 word-stems associated with each of the 25 topics estimated by my primary LDA model in Tables 1 and 2 below... The column labels were assigned by the author to give a general sense of how one would characterize each cluster of words.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: In this respect, the appearance of these topics offers some additional support for Hypothesis 1, which posited that the UNFCCC's diverse and inclusive nature would induce climate change negotiations to encompass an issue-space that extends well beyond the core climate change issue-dimensions emphasized by the UNFCCC and others.
components.downstream_description: The 25 topics from the LDA model are qualitatively interpreted to map the issue-space of climate negotiations. This interpretation is used as evidence to support the hypothesis that negotiations are topically complex and extend beyond core issues.
components.downstream_evidence: Turning to these tables, one can first note that the 25 topics identified by LDA generally appear to correspond to meaningful negotiation issues. However, the correspondence between the latent topics and the core, most widely accepted, dimensions of global climate change cooperation-as defined by the UNFCCCfoot_13 or related research (Weiler 2012;Bailer and Weiler 2014) foot_14 -is mixed.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: Figure 2 provides strong support for the corollary to Hypothesis 1: even after reducing the 25 topic LDA model's resultant topic-space down to the most dominant topic for each climate speech, a large and diverse number of latent topics continues to dominate the UNFCCC's overall climate change negotiation process.
components.downstream_description: A single 'dominant topic' is assigned to each speech document based on the highest topic proportion. The frequency distribution of these dominant topics is then plotted and analyzed to show that negotiation complexity remains high even after this dimension reduction.
components.downstream_evidence: Hence, I next seek to better identify the primary bargaining space for my COPs of interest by determining the dominant underlying topic for each and every speech-document. I do so by identifying the topic whose associated words represent the highest proportion of all words found within a given speech-document, which I then assign as the dominant topic for that document, and repeat this process for each speech.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: In this manner, I am assuming that the latent topic that a country devotes the majority of its speech to will be the bargaining topic that is most valued by that country within international climate change negotiations.
components.downstream_description: The dominant topic assignments are used to compare the topical diversity of different subgroups of countries ('clubs'). The distributions of dominant topics for these clubs are plotted, and a Fisher's Exact Test is used to assess if the reduction in topical diversity is statistically significant.
components.downstream_evidence: To reevaluate the dimensionality of climate negotiations under the smaller clubs listed above, I thus reduce my speechcorpus down to match the specific set of countries in each club, and then re-plot the distribution of my previously assigned dominant topics for each club.

downstream.downstream_description: The study uses a 25-topic LDA model to analyze climate negotiation speeches. First, the topics are qualitatively interpreted to argue for the high complexity of the negotiation space (Hypothesis 1). Second, a dominant topic is assigned to each speech to create a reduced representation of the data, which is analyzed via frequency plots to show complexity persists. Third, these dominant topic assignments are used to compare topical diversity across different 'clubs' of countries, using frequency plots and a Fisher's Exact Test to show that smaller clubs significantly reduce negotiation complexity (Hypothesis 2).

Following their methodology, we established the minimum topic size required for effective clustering sentences to be 49. In the end, 104 topics were identified.

The findings are presented in tables and interpreted to understand the alignment and misalignment between scientific research and policy priorities.

downstream.downstream_description: The study uses BERTopic to identify topics in two corpora (Chilean sustainability research articles and government environmental policies). The primary downstream analysis involves calculating and comparing the prevalence of these topics across the two corpora to identify 'gaps' or misalignments. A 'gap' score is calculated for each topic, and the results are used to discuss which themes are more prominent in research versus policy, thereby evaluating the science-policy interface.

data.data_source_detail: The study uses a World Bank dataset of loan conditions, or "prior actions", from 1985 to 2014. It also analyzes the collection of research papers published by the World Bank.
data.preprocessing_details: We minimally pre-process all text by excluding country names, country-specific abbreviations, months, generic verbs, and terms in languages other than English. For the research papers, they also remove numbers, punctuation, and proper nouns, then tokenize the text.

model.algorithm_detail: The study uses a keyword-assisted topic model (keyATM).
model.K_evidence: In the next step, we estimate a dynamic keyword-assisted topic model with six topics specified with the keywords plus one keyword-less topic.

K_selection.selection_evidence: We draw a random sample of 200 texts from the loan conditionsfoot_6 and apply a keyword-free latent Dirichlet allocation model to these texts, iterating through different topic numbers from one to ten, and inspect the words most probably associated with each topic. This process serves to uncover the language and terminology used in World Bank loans but does not otherwise influence whether topics actually appear in the subsequent analysis. We then choose 12-18 keywords to identify each topic, listed in Table 1.

labeling.labeling_evidence: The keyATM model requires that researchers specify meaningful keywords ex ante to distinguish topics.
labeling.label_validation_evidence: Most of the words appear substantively related to the topics, indicating that our topics are indeed well identified by the model and highly interpretable.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: To our knowledge, this is the first study to quantify the Bank's internal economic research and policy priorities, measure how they change over time, and use that measure in a quantitative study to show the ways in which shifts in Bank ideas affect key aspects of Bank lending.
components.downstream_description: The prevalence of each of six topics in World Bank loan conditions is used as a dependent variable in a series of regression models. The models test whether the lagged prevalence of the same topic in the Bank's research output, along with other country-level covariates, predicts the topic's prevalence in loan conditions.
components.downstream_evidence: The summary finding is that, with a lag, Bank research on a topic shapes the prevalence of that topic in Bank loan conditions at least as much as any other factor central to models of Bank lending.

downstream.downstream_description: The study uses the prevalence of six topics in World Bank loan conditions as dependent variables in a series of regression models. The primary goal is to assess the influence of the Bank's own research agenda (measured as the lagged prevalence of the same topics in research documents) on the types of conditions it imposes, controlling for various borrower country characteristics. The results are presented topic-by-topic, with standardized coefficients plotted to compare effect sizes.

metadata.title: Application of Natural Language Processing to the electoral manifestos of parties characterised by populist rhetoric in Central and Eastern Europe

data.data_source_detail: We apply the above-mentioned techniques on manifestos of parties characterised by populist rhetoric over the 2000-2022 period. The data and the categorization come from the MARPOR dataset (Volkens et al., 2019), specifically from the 2023-1 version of the corpus.
data.preprocessing_details: For each document used we remove stop words by removing the 1149 stop words already categorised in English in the R Tidytext package and we also remove the dictionary words followed or anticipated by a negation using a regex expression. Following the translation, we perform tokenization, stemming, lemmatization, and other preprocessing steps necessary for analysing our corpus.

model.algorithm_detail: The text mentions both STM and LDA: "In our analysis, we employ Structural Topic Modelling (STM) with LDA..." but later clarifies "Specifically, we use LDA due to its interpretability and its well-established status in the literature." and reports results as from LDA.
model.K_evidence: We experiment with a range of values, evaluate their performance against these metrics, and select the number of topics that achieves an optimal balance between model fit and interpretability, which is five intersections. east european journal of society and politics, 10 (4): 6-33. in this case.

K_selection.selection_evidence: We determine the optimal number of topics by integrating both quantitative metrics and qualitative evaluations. Quantitative metrics such as log-likelihood, perplexity, and topic coherence offered objective measures of model performance, while qualitative assessments focus on the interpretability of the resulting topics. We experiment with a range of values, evaluate their performance against these metrics, and select the number of topics that achieves an optimal balance between model fit and interpretability, which is five intersections. east european journal of society and politics, 10 (4): 6-33. in this case.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: This study aimed to examine the rhetoric employed by parties characterized by populist tendencies in Central and Eastern Europe (CEE), comparing them with non-populist counterparts and distinguishing between 'centrist populists' and 'radical populists'. Our analysis, utilizing Natural Language Processing techniques such as sentiment analysis, n-grams, and topic modelling, reveals complex patterns in the political discourse across the region.
components.downstream_description: The topic model is used to identify the main themes in political party manifestos. The analysis then involves a qualitative interpretation of these topics and a comparison of the topics prevalent among different groups of parties (populist vs. non-populist, centrist vs. radical) and across various Central and Eastern European countries to identify commonalities and differences in their political rhetoric.
components.downstream_evidence: Our Topic Modelling analysis, using Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), reveals further nuances in the discourse of different party groups (Table 8). Non-populist parties across countries generally focus on conventional political topics such as economic, social, and infrastructure development.

downstream.downstream_description: The study uses topic modeling to identify and interpret the thematic content of political party manifestos. The primary downstream analysis consists of a qualitative comparison of these topics across different party types (populist vs. non-populist; centrist vs. radical populist) and across several Central and Eastern European countries to map the landscape of political rhetoric and identify convergent and divergent themes.

paper_id: Cross and Greene - 2020 - Talk is not cheap Policy agendas, information processing, and the unusually proportional nature of

metadata.title: Talk is not cheap: Policy agendas, information processing, and the unusually proportional nature of European Central Bank communications policy responses
keywords: and Derek Greene. "Talk Is Not Cheap: Policy Agendas
and the Unusually Proportional Nature of European Central Bank Communications Policy Cross
D. Talk is not cheap: Policy agendas
and the unusually proportional nature of European Central Bank communications policy responses. Governance

data.data_source_detail: ECB Governing Council speeches
data.preprocessing_details: The topic model here elaborates upon that implemented by Greene and Cross (2017) by including n-grams in our document representation -i.e, contiguous sequences of n words from a given document. We apply the pre-processing steps descried in Online Appendix A to each of the 78 quarterly "time windows" represented in the overall corpus.

model.algorithm_detail: The study uses a dynamic topic modeling approach based on Greene and Cross (2017), which generates topics for each time window independently. A footnote specifies it is an NMF-based approach.
model.K_evidence: To assess the inter-topic semantic validity of the results, we examine the extent to any meaningful higher-level clusters exist among the 56 dynamic topics.

labeling.labeling_evidence: Our first task is to identify the substantive policy area that each topic relates to so that an appropriate topic label can be manually assigned. This is done by considering the topic descriptors associated with each topic (here corresponding to the top ranked terms, including relevant n-grams), and examining how they change over time.
labeling.label_validation_evidence: To assess the inter-topic semantic validity of the results, we examine the extent to any meaningful higher-level clusters exist among the 56 dynamic topics. ... The fact that these higher-level clusters make intuitive sense given substantive knowledge of what the ECB does provides evidence that our dynamic topic-modeling approach is capturing semantically valid topics.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: we provide a broad overview of the latent policy themes revealed in our corpus by our dynamic topic model.
components.validation_evidence: The fact that these higher-level clusters make intuitive sense given substantive knowledge of what the ECB does provides evidence that our dynamic topic-modeling approach is capturing semantically valid topics.
components.downstream_description: Authors interpret and validate 56 dynamic topics by clustering them into 10 higher-level groupings and assessing their semantic coherence and face validity based on substantive knowledge.
components.downstream_evidence: To assess the inter-topic semantic validity of the results, we examine the extent to any meaningful higher-level clusters exist among the 56 dynamic topics.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: Figure 2 shows how attention to different topic categories varies over time.
components.downstream_description: Authors plot the attention given to different topic categories over time to show how the ECB's policy agenda has shifted, noting that some topics are stable while others are crisis-dependent.
components.downstream_evidence: Figure 2 shows how attention to different topic categories varies over time. Topic categories such as the 'Real economy' and the 'Financial system' are regularly discussed across the entire period, whereas crisis-related topic categories such as 'Bank balance sheets' are much more prominent during the apex of the Eurocrisis from 2012.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: Our focus is on the first differences in the percentages of speech paragraphs dedicated to a given policy
components.downstream_description: Authors calculate L-kurtosis statistics from the first differences of topic proportions (percentage of paragraphs) to test the punctuated equilibrium hypothesis. They compare kurtosis across topic categories, between the ECB and national central banks, and against other policy systems.
components.downstream_evidence: When we look at the L-kurtosis statistics for each of the topic-categories, some interesting variation emerges. The topic-categories relating to 'European integration' and the 'Role of Central Banks' exhibit less kurtosis than a Gaussian normal distribution, while topic categories we would expect to exhibit punctuations like the 'Eurocrisis' and 'Banking supervision' are more leptokurtic 7 .

downstream.downstream_description: The study uses a dynamic topic model to first identify and validate latent policy themes in ECB communications through qualitative interpretation and hierarchical clustering. It then plots the prevalence of these topic clusters over time to describe agenda shifts. The core analysis uses the first differences of topic proportions to calculate L-kurtosis statistics, testing the punctuated equilibrium hypothesis by comparing distributions across topics, central banks, and other policy systems.

paper_id: De Marchi et al. - 2021 - Policy and the structure of roll call voting in the US house

metadata.title: Policy and the structure of roll call voting in the US house

data.data_source_detail: full text of bills before Congress
data.corpus_size_raw: In the 111th House, there are 297 final passage votes; in the 112th, there are 274 and for the 113th, there are 293

model.algorithm_detail: The paper mentions using LDA but ultimately chooses STM to deal with multiple local optima and for ease of replication.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: Building off of recent research, our main hypothesis is that measures of bill content based on a structural topics model will be most effective in forecasting legislator behaviour.
components.validation_evidence: In our model, accuracy is approximately 94% out-of-sample for the two Congresses when the Democrats are in the minority.
components.downstream_description: Topic proportions are used as independent variables in regularized logistic regression models to predict roll call votes. The analysis evaluates the out-of-sample predictive accuracy of the model and identifies the most important variables, finding that topics are the strongest predictors.
components.downstream_evidence: In short, the answer is that the topic variables, followed at some considerable distance by the committee variables, are doing the most work in predicting roll calls.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: Given the strength of our model in predicting votes and the dominant role topics play in this model, we have an opportunity to investigate the substantive areas of legislation that most incline the minority party to either support or try to defeat legislation.
components.downstream_description: The marginal effects of each topic from the regression models are compared by political party (majority vs. minority) across three different congressional terms to identify the substantive topics that lead to partisan agreement or disagreement.
components.downstream_evidence: Our approach is to examine party behaviour by comparing the marginal effects of each topic by party, which indicates whether the party supports (or not) legislation in that substantive area.

downstream.downstream_description: The study uses topic variables from bill texts as predictors in regularized regression models to forecast roll call votes. The analysis first demonstrates the superior predictive power of bill topics compared to traditional measures like ideology. Subsequently, it uses the model's outputs (marginal effects of topics) to identify the specific legislative areas that drive partisan disagreement and consensus, thereby analyzing political polarization across different Congresses and majority/minority party configurations.

paper_id: De Slegte et al. - 2025 - The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science An In-Depth Literature Review

metadata.title: The Use of Machine Learning Methods in Political Science: An In-Depth Literature Review

downstream.downstream_description: The provided text is a systematic review of how machine learning methods are used in political science. The authors' analysis involves categorizing and counting published articles based on methods, data types, and research purposes. The paper does not conduct its own topic modeling analysis on a corpus of text to generate findings. It reports that topic modeling is a prevalent method used in the articles it reviewed, but does not present or analyze any topic model outputs as part of its own results.

paper_id: De Vries et al. - 2018 - No Longer Lost in Translation Evidence that Google Translate Works for Comparative Bag-of-Words Tex

metadata.title: No Longer Lost in Translation: Evidence that Google Translate Works for Comparative Bag-of-Words Text Applications

data.data_source_detail: The europarl dataset (Koehn 2005) contains parallel corpora which allow us to set up such comparisons. The dataset consists of official European Parliament debate transcriptions in most of the official EU languages.
data.corpus_size_raw: between 2148 (DE) and 2347 (FR) chapters per language pair
data.preprocessing_details: we have removed punctuation, numbers and general stopwords, and all remaining words have been lowercased and stemmed.

model.algorithm_detail: LDA algorithm (Blei, Ng, and Jordan 2003) and Gibbs sampling.
model.K_evidence: Therefore, we settled for 90 topics.

K_selection.selection_evidence: We estimated the best-fitting number of topics by evaluating the model harmonic mean of models that contain between 10 and 150 topics, in increments of 10. The model harmonic mean indicates the extent to which word counts in the documents used to construct the model match the word distributions in the model itself. Put differently, it indicates the extent to which the model accurately describes the distribution of words in the documents. In this case, a larger harmonic mean indicates that the model fits the data better. The results of the optimization runs are displayed in Figure 2. The gold standard model has an optimum of 90 topics.

labeling.labeling_evidence: Our matching procedure is as follows: for each stem we find the highest loading in the machine-translated topic model and the gold standard topic model. For example, take the stem "agricultur". This stem loads highest on (is most important in) topic 12 of the machine-translated model, and topic 45 in the gold standard model. This results in a 12-45 topic pairing for that specific stem.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: we report three comparisons based on our topic models, all of which give us evidence on how much the matched topics in the machine-translated and the gold standard topics overlap in content and prevalence.
components.validation_evidence: we report three comparisons based on our topic models, all of which give us evidence on how much the matched topics in the machine-translated and the gold standard topics overlap in content and prevalence.
components.downstream_description: The study compares topic models generated from machine-translated texts against those from 'gold standard' translations to validate the use of machine translation. The comparison is performed on three levels: topic prevalence per document pair, topic prevalence across the corpus for matched topics, and the content (stem loadings) of matched topics. Similarity is primarily measured using correlation.
components.downstream_evidence: Do the topic models with the machine-translated text as input assign the same topics to a document as the topic models with the gold standard translated texts? Figure 5 displays for each language how similar topical prevalence is for each pair of gold standard and machine-translated documents (based on an equal number of topics; for the comparison between unequal number of topics, see the Appendix).

downstream.downstream_description: The study conducts a methodological validation of using machine-translated text for topic modeling. It compares topic models from machine-translated corpora against those from 'gold standard' human-translated corpora across several languages. The analysis involves three comparisons: the similarity of topic prevalence per document, the similarity of topic prevalence across the entire corpus, and the similarity of topic content (stem loadings), using correlation as the primary metric.

paper_id: Debus and Florczak - 2022 - Using party press releases and Wikipedia page view data to analyse developments and determinants of

metadata.title: Using party press releases and Wikipedia page view data to analyse developments and determinants of parties’ issue prevalence: Evidence for the right-wing populist ‘Alternative for Germany’

data.data_source_detail: Our data consist of 2262 press releases collected in the spring of 2020. We collected all data back to 2016 from the current party website. However, our database covers also press releases from the now decommissioned AfD website that existed from 2013 to the end of 2015.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: The aim of this paper was not only to demonstrate that measuring the programmatic profile of parties on the basis of their daily press releases works and can help to identify changes in the parties' policy profile in a more nuanced manner but also to shed light on the degree to which parties react to issue prevalence among the public.
components.downstream_description: The average proportions of four key topics (Eurozone, economic policy, refugees/borders, migration) are aggregated by year and analyzed descriptively to show changes in their prevalence over time.
components.downstream_evidence: We perform descriptive bivariate analyses of the average topic proportions over time aggregated at the year level for the four topics of interest. Figure 2 below shows that both the Eurozone and economic policy topics decreased in relevance over time, whereasas expectedrefugee related topics became more frequent in AfD press releases, although only significantly for the first topic related to migration issues ('Refugees and Borders').

components.epistemic_function_evidence: The aim of this paper was not only to demonstrate that measuring the programmatic profile of parties on the basis of their daily press releases works and can help to identify changes in the parties' policy profile in a more nuanced manner but also to shed light on the degree to which parties react to issue prevalence among the public.
components.downstream_description: Estimated topic proportions for four topics are used as dependent variables in fractional probit regression models to test if they are predicted by public interest, as measured by daily hits on related German Wikipedia pages.
components.downstream_evidence: The dependent variables are therefore the estimated topic proportions for the topics 'EU and Merkel's Role in the Refugee Crisis', 'Refugees and Borders', 'Financial Policy' and 'Eurozone', while our main explanatory variables are the numbers of hits in the German Wikipedia that we selected as connected to the content of the topics

downstream.downstream_description: The study uses topic proportions from press releases in two ways. First, it descriptively analyzes and plots the average annual proportions of four key topics to show changes in the party's issue focus over time. Second, it uses these topic proportions as dependent variables in fractional probit regression models to test whether the party's daily issue attention is associated with public interest, measured via Wikipedia page hits.

metadata.journal: JCMS: Journal of Common Market Studies

data.data_source_detail: Print media articles from quality newspapers and tabloids in six countries: Spain, Denmark, Sweden, France, UK and Ireland.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: Through expounding the most salient issues discussed in news articles containing the words eurosceptic and Euroscepticism during 2014, we found a strong convergence in topics in five of the six cases studied.
components.validation_evidence: This human coding of the articles' context helps validate the LDA models, where our labelling of generated topics also rendered 70 per cent as European in scope.

downstream.downstream_description: The study uses LDA topic modeling to analyze media articles on euroscepticism across six countries. paper_id: Dybowski and Adämmer - 2018 - The economic effects of U.S. presidential tax communication Evidence from a correlated topic model

metadata.title: The economic effects of U.S. presidential tax communication: Evidence from a correlated topic model
keywords: C32 C82 D72 D83 E61 Tax policy U

data.data_source_detail: Our source for presidential communication is The American Presidency Project, an online resource dedicated to the study of the U.S. Presidency by Woolley and Peters (2017). It contains an extensive collection of presidential transcripts, such as presidential speeches, radio addresses, State of the Union addresses, inaugural speeches, press conferences and statements from the White House.
data.preprocessing_details: To reduce complexity, we pre-process all documents according to standard routines in computational linguistics. We separate words by delimiting characters such as hyphens or apostrophes. We also remove words with less than three letters, words that belong to a standard stoplist (i.e., articles, conjunctions or common functional words such as the, also, or because), and words that occur only once in the corpus. Individual characters and numbers are removed as well.

model.K_evidence: The remaining task for the researcher is to choose an adequate number of topics, which we set to 100.

K_selection.selection_evidence: Our choice reflects the careful examination of a trade-off: If the present number of topics is too small, topics are potentially inflated and too general for specific analyses. By the same token, setting the number of topics too large results in an over-fitting, which causes a subdivision of one topic into multiple related topics, hindering their interpretation (DiMaggio et al., 2013). Since our aim is to find the best fit for the latent topics contained in the corpus, we ran the algorithm with 50, 75, 100, and 150 topics on our corpus. We find that a calibration of 100 topics yields the best-behaved results with respect to the outlined trade-off.

labeling.labeling_evidence: It is up to the researcher to make meaning of each topic based on the coherence of words it contains with high probability (DiMaggio et al., 2013). Accordingly, we add the following headlines to the eight topics depicted in Table 2: tax policy, environment, legislation, political parties, health care, crime, drugs, and labor market.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: We fill this gap in the literature by combining a probabilistic topic model and a dictionary-based sentiment analysis to construct a measure that captures the salience and tone of U.S. presidential tax communication since 1945.
components.downstream_description: The Tax News Sentiment Measure (TNSM) is used as a variable in a Structural Vector Autoregression (SVAR) model to estimate its dynamic effects on GDP, private consumption, and private investment, presenting the results as impulse response functions.
components.downstream_evidence: To achieve identification with inclusion of the TNSM (N t ) and the implicit tax rate (S t ), we follow arguments by Leeper et al. (2013b) and make the following assumptions: Tax policy news have no immediate impact on current tax revenues (a N = 0), government spending (b N = 0) and output (c N = 0).

components.epistemic_function_evidence: We fill this gap in the literature by combining a probabilistic topic model and a dictionary-based sentiment analysis to construct a measure that captures the salience and tone of U.S. presidential tax communication since 1945.
components.downstream_description: The TNSM is used in an extended SVAR model that includes a consumer sentiment index to test whether confidence acts as a transmission channel for the effect of presidential tax news on economic output.
components.downstream_evidence: To investigate whether the TNSM influences output via such sentiment effects, we extend our previous econometric framework with the most important and widely used confidence measure, i.e., the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index (see e.g., Barsky and Sims, 2011;Bachmann and Sims, 2012;Angeletos et al., 2016).

downstream.downstream_description: The paper constructs a Tax News Sentiment Measure (TNSM) from a topic model and sentiment analysis. This measure is then used as a key variable in a series of Structural Vector Autoregression (SVAR) models. The primary analyses estimate the dynamic effects of the TNSM on macroeconomic outcomes like GDP, consumption, and investment. A subsequent analysis extends the model to test if consumer confidence is a transmission channel for the observed effects.

paper_id: Dyevre and Lampach - 2021 - Issue attention on international courts Evidence from the European Court of Justice

metadata.title: Issue attention on international courts: Evidence from the European Court of Justice

data.data_source_detail: all published ECJ decisions, up to 31 December 2015, from the EUR-Lex website.
data.preprocessing_details: The raw documents were subject to the conventional pre-processing steps of removing punctuation, numbers, html tags, functional words and words appearing in less than five decisions before converting each corpus into a document-term matrix. ... we also removed the issue codes from the judgements.

downstream.downstream_description: The authors use a topic model to identify eight key themes in the literature on big data analytics in public policy. First, we measured and then compared the semantic coherence and exclusivity of models ranging from 10 to 20 topics (see Table 1 in the Supplementary Information). Semantic coherence measures whether the terms in a topic model appear with a high degree of terminological cooccurrence, whereas exclusivity measures the level of differentiation between the topics. The results from these tests show that a 15-topic model has the second highest semantic coherence (-85.10190) and a higher exclusivity (9.623557) than the 10-topic model. ... When combined, these results define a topic according to both the salience of the terms that compose it and those terms that most distinguish one topic from the other topics present in the model. We gave each topic a name on this basis.

These shares are then used as dependent variables in a series of regression models to test their association with macroeconomic factors like unemployment and inflation, with various controls and robustness checks. Second, the study presents the topics themselves as findings, interpreting their meaning and illustrating them with verbatim quotes from the speeches to provide a qualitative map of the 'political voice' on monetary policy.

The main DiD analysis then compares how the prevalence of these topics in parliamentary speeches changed for MPs from the most oil-dependent region versus MPs from other regions, finding that the shock led MPs from the affected region to shift their focus away from environmental topics and towards unemployment.

data.preprocessing_details: Text pre-processing includes lowercase conversion and the deletion of punctuation, numbers and English stop words, as well as lemmatisation, i.e., the process of reducing words to their base or root form (lemma). Further manual custom-word removal, aiming to exclude from the analysis frequently recurring words involves: words with fewer than three characters, the possessive case ('s), modal verbs (may, must, shall, have to, need, will), party names (PPE, S&D, Renew, etc.), speaker's qualifications and roles (mr, mrs, president, commissioner, etc.), forms of address and courtesy (ladies and gentlemen, dear, honourable, etc.).

K_selection.selection_evidence: To evaluate our model, prior to the analysis, a number K of expected topics is also defined by means of a trade-off method (Mimno et al., 2011;Silge, 2018) between word exclusivitythe unlikelihood of the most frequent words in one topic to overlap with those in anotherand semantic coherenceco-occurrence of high-probability words in the same topic. By plotting all the results from the model testing a range of Ks from 10 to 50 (Figure 1), 21k, 22k, 28k, 29k and 36k seem to be the numbers of topics showing the best balance between high coherence and high exclusivity, with 28k performing slightly better than the others.

labeling.labeling_evidence: Finally, the interpretation and labelling of the 28 topics triangulates an analysis of the highestprobability and most frequent and exclusive words with the use of the findThoughts function from the STM package, which displays the most representative interventions for each topic (Table 1 in the Annexes).

They interpret the topics and compare their prevalence across the two corpora to argue that Ukrainian refugees are framed with more solidarity and victimhood (e.g., topics on women and children), while Syrian refugees are framed in terms of security, religion (Islam/terrorism), and as an anonymous 'flow' or 'crisis'.
components.downstream_evidence: Several considerations made at a word level, are confirmed by the frequency distribution of bigrams (Figures 4 and 5) including the terms refugee(s), asylum, (im)migration and (im)migrant(s) and by the STM analysis (in Figure 6 the proportions in which interventions from the Syria and Ukraine datasets contribute to shaping each topic).

downstream.downstream_description: The authors use a Structural Topic Model (STM) as a primary method for comparing the discursive framing of Ukrainian and Syrian refugees in the European Parliament. The analysis involves interpreting the meaning of topics and comparing their prevalence across the two corpora (Ukraine vs. Syria) to identify differences in themes such as victimhood, gender, religion, and security. This comparative thematic analysis forms the core of the paper's findings, arguing for a differential, and potentially discriminatory, representation of the two refugee groups.

model.algorithm_detail: The paper develops and uses a custom model called the 'expressed agenda model', described as a hierarchical mixture model where mixture weights (senators' expressed agendas) vary across senators, but the components (topics) are fixed. It uses a von Mises-Fisher (vMF) distribution for documents.

K_selection.selection_evidence: I varied the number of assumed topic from only five topics, up to 85 different topics. Assuming too few topics resulted in distinct issue being lumped together, whereas too many topics results in several clusters referring to the same issues. During my tests, 43 issues represented a decent middle ground. I corroborated this number of clusters using a nonparametric model for text clustering, based upon the Dirichlet process prior. This model identified 40-45 clusters in the data set under a wide range of hyperpriors (Blei and Lafferty 2006).

labeling.labeling_evidence: Two methods were used to label these topics. First, I used topics with appropriations-related labels and increased attention around the passage of an appropriation bill. components.downstream_description: The model's outputs, specifically the proportion of press releases dedicated to appropriations topics (both a single topic and an aggregated measure), are used as predictor variables in a probit regression to predict senators' votes on an earmark reform amendment.
components.downstream_evidence: Figure 6 displays the relationship between senators' vote on the Demint-McCain amendment and two components of the expressed agenda: the proportion of press releases allocated to discussing fire department grants (left-hand plot) and a composite measure of appropriations (center-and right-hand plots).

components.epistemic_function_evidence: The expressed agenda model and the Senate press releases allow a direct and comprehensive test of whether senators from the same state articulate a distinctive set of priorities in press releases.
components.downstream_description: To test a hypothesis about legislative strategy, a distance metric is calculated between the full topic proportion vectors ('expressed agendas') of pairs of senators. The analysis then compares the average distance for senators from the same state versus senators from different states to determine if same-state senators have more or less similar agendas.
components.downstream_evidence: To measure the distance between two expressed agendas, I use the distance metric on the simplex defined in Billheimer et al. (2001), which generalizes intuition about properties of distance in Euclidean space to the simplex. In Appendix 14 I define this metric.

downstream.downstream_description: The paper uses the outputs of an 'expressed agenda model' (topic proportions) for several downstream tasks. First, it validates the model by showing that committee leaders dedicate more attention to their committee's topics than other senators. Second, it validates the model by demonstrating that topic attention clusters geographically as expected (e.g., Western senators on public lands). Third, it uses topic proportions as predictor variables in a probit regression to show that attention to appropriations predicts votes on earmark reform. Finally, it tests a substantive hypothesis by calculating a distance metric between senators' full topic proportion vectors to compare the agenda similarity of same-state versus different-state senators.

downstream.downstream_description: The authors use topic modeling outputs in two main ways. First, they analyze 'topic ownership' by calculating the frequency of dominant topics for different types of actors (media, political) and ideological groups. Second, they use the full topic proportion vectors for each actor to construct actor-actor networks based on topic usage similarity. These networks are then analyzed using community detection, centrality measures, and network percolation techniques to assess the fragmentation of the political debate across three countries and the different roles media and political actors play in this process.

downstream.downstream_description: The study first uses topic modeling to inductively discover themes in Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs). components.downstream_description: A measure of issue engagement, derived from the STM, is used as the dependent variable in logistic regression models to test how communication channel (Twitter vs. press releases), ideological distance, party size, and government participation influence the likelihood of engagement.
components.downstream_evidence: In the next step, I use statistical models to dive deeper into the dynamics of issue engagement in press releases and tweets. Table 3 provides the results of logistic regression models investigating how Twitter influences issue engagement between parties and the (potential) interaction effects with ideological distance, party size and government participation.

downstream.downstream_description: The authors use a Structural Topic Model (STM) to identify political issues (topics) in party communications (press releases and tweets). From this, they construct a measure of "issue engagement," defined as one party addressing an issue recently discussed by another. This derived measure is then analyzed in three ways: 1) descriptively, by comparing counts of engagement across communication channels, countries, and issues; 2) structurally, by creating and comparing networks of inter-party engagement; and 3) statistically, by using it as the dependent variable in logistic regression models to test hypotheses about the influence of communication channel, party size, government status, and ideology on engagement patterns.

components.downstream_evidence: The results from the regression analyses corroborate the descriptive findings. In Figure 3, we display the average marginal effects (AMEs) of the election year on the respective topic proportions. A positive AME indicates that this topic had become more prevalent for a party in the 2017 election compared to in the 2013 election.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: Our approach is similar to that of Bauer et al. (2017), that is, we analyze whether the placement of respondents on the left-right scale depends on their interpretations of 'left' and 'right'.
components.downstream_description: The proportion of the 'Racism' topic is used as a primary independent variable in a regression model to predict candidates' self-placement on the left-right scale, controlling for party.
components.downstream_evidence: Our main independent variable was the proportion for 'Racism'. This variable ranged between zero (for candidates who did not mention this topic at all) and one (for candidates who addressed this topic exclusively). If it is irrelevant how candidates interpret the term 'right', this variable should not correlate with the left-right placement; however, if this variable has a significant and substantially relevant coefficient, the left-right scale is influenced by how candidates interpret these terms.

downstream.downstream_description: The study uses topic modeling outputs in a multi-stage analysis. First, topics are qualitatively interpreted and labeled, and their overall prevalence is described. Second, topic proportions are descriptively compared across political parties and between two election years. Third, these descriptive comparisons are corroborated using regression models where topic proportions are the dependent variable and election year is the independent variable. Finally, the proportion of a specific topic ('Racism') is used as an independent variable in a separate regression analysis to predict candidates' ideological self-placement.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: Overall, the textual analysis, as a variant of "computational abductive analysis" (Karell & Freedman, 2019), points to the importance of political implications of mainland immigration, which prompts us to highlight democratic values in our theoretical framework and pay closer attention to priming shared democratic values in our subsequent experiment.

First, they perform a qualitative interpretation of the five discovered topics, using exemplar news articles to illustrate their meanings. Second, they analyze the prevalence of these topics over time, identifying a shift in media focus from socio-economic to political issues. Third, they use topic prevalence as an outcome variable in a regression discontinuity in time (RDiT) model to causally assess the impact of political events on media coverage. Finally, the insights from this textual analysis are used to motivate the theoretical framework and design of a subsequent survey experiment.

downstream.downstream_description: The study uses topic modeling outputs in three main ways. paper_id: Koo - 2022 - Global perceptions of South Korea's COVID-19 policy responses Topic modeling with tweets

metadata.title: Global perceptions of South Korea's COVID-19 policy responses: Topic modeling with tweets

data.data_source_detail: Twitter data collected using the Twitter API v1 accessed through the Academic Research product track.
data.preprocessing_details: I had to remove all the metadata, including the https hyperlink, and short words of fewer than three characters. Hashtags were also removed... I processed each tweet text to remove all punctuation marks, numbers, stopwords, and web addresses, and further implemented lower-casing, stemming, and tokenization.

model.algorithm_detail: PTM (Pseudo-document Topic Model), which uses implicit aggregation of short texts.
model.software_detail: Tomotopy package in Python
model.K_evidence: I settled with a solution that generated 35 topics with a coherence value score of 3,529.99 and a perplexity value score of .73, a best fitting model characterizing interpretability of the identified topics as well as predictability of the model (Bagozzi and Berliner, 2018;Syed and Spruit, 2017).

K_selection.selection_evidence: I ran several different experiments varying the number of topics from 15 to 45, with 5 as the step unit, and selected the final model in consideration of both the highest coherence values measuring semantic similarity between high scoring words in the topic and the lowest perplexity values measuring how well the selected model predicts a sample. I settled with a solution that generated 35 topics with a coherence value score of 3,529.99 and a perplexity value score of .73, a best fitting model characterizing interpretability of the identified topics as well as predictability of the model (Bagozzi and Berliner, 2018;Syed and Spruit, 2017).

labeling.labeling_evidence: Table 1 shows the 35-topic solution, listing the 20 highest-ranked terms for each topic, the labels corresponding to the terms of each topic and the topic's reference number.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: These 35 topics from the final model are the best characterizations of the collected corpus of COVID-19 tweets.
components.downstream_description: The authors structure their results by grouping 35 topics into thematic clusters. For each theme and its constituent topics, they provide qualitative interpretation based on top words and illustrate the findings with exemplar tweets selected for having the highest topic scores or probabilities. This approach is used to describe and compare the content of public discussion on Twitter regarding various aspects of the pandemic in South Korea, including human rights, economic impacts, and government responses.
components.downstream_evidence: The topic of fatalities in the United States, for example, included the words population, country, Trump, United States, president, test, world, China, Germany, Italy, and fatality. These terms reflect a clear concern about COVID-related fatalities in countries around the world, including the United States. One tweet with the largest topic score under this topic went as follows: "South Korea, population 51 million. South Korea has had 2,073 covid deaths. The US, in reality, has around 800,000. How do you explain this?"

downstream.downstream_description: The authors use a 35-topic model to structure their entire analysis of tweets about COVID-19 in South Korea. They group topics into broader themes (e.g., countries' responses, vaccines, human rights), interpret each topic's meaning, and use exemplar tweets—selected based on high topic scores/probabilities—to provide qualitative evidence. The analysis compares the relative attention given to different issues and uses the topic model's findings to evaluate the public discourse surrounding South Korea's pandemic response.

paper_id: Kowalski et al. - 2020 - Improving public services by mining citizen feedback An application of natural language processing

data.data_source_detail: online reviews about publicly funded primary care (GP) services in England, accompanied by 5-point Likert-scale star ratings.
data.preprocessing_details: The present study lower-cased and stemmed the tokens (words) and removed numbers, punctuation, stop words, tokens shorter than three characters and tokens that appeared fewer than 10 times or more than 100,000 times in the corpus.

model.K_evidence: Our analysis found that 20 topics was the optimal setting for our objective: to evaluate how well textual comments could be analysed through machine learning, for use in public policy research.

K_selection.selection_evidence: We followed Roberts et al. (2015) in selecting the optimal number of topics by balancing exclusivity and semantic coherence in models ranging between three and 100 topics.

labeling.labeling_evidence: The topics drawn from the 20-topic STM model were labelled in accordance with the most common words they contained.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: The topic map illustrates, on a two-dimensional plane, how similar 20 topics generated via the STM topic model from NHS GP practice reviews are to one another.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: In the present study, they provided a well-established template measure, related to the metrics of user satisfaction derived from topic modelling.
components.downstream_description: Topic proportions are used as independent variables in a Random Forest model to predict six different Likert-scale user satisfaction ratings. The feature importance from the model is then interpreted to identify which topics are the strongest predictors of satisfaction.
components.downstream_evidence: We began by estimating Random Forest (RF) models, in which the proportional presence of topic reviews constituted independent variables and six Likert-scale ratings were treated as dependent variables.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: For the sake of simplicity, topic proportions were grouped into negative, neutral and positive clusters, as indicated by the symbols scheme in Figure 1. The percentage of positive and negative topics in comments was used as an independent variable in fixed-effects models.
components.validation_evidence: While we could not access external data to validate these results, this expected direction of coefficients on positive and negative topic-cluster variables, when other covariates were controlled for, represented a weak form of validation.
components.downstream_description: For a robustness analysis, topic proportions are clustered into positive and negative groups. The percentage of these topic clusters in reviews is used as an independent variable in a two-way (CCG and month) fixed-effects regression model to predict star ratings, controlling for other variables.
components.downstream_evidence: Fixed-effects models were used to establish whether the statistically significant correlations between topics identified in the textual comments and star ratings continued to hold after other relevant variables were controlled for.

downstream.downstream_description: The study uses topic model outputs in three main ways. First, it visualizes the relationships between topics using a 2D topic map. Second, it uses the proportional presence of 20 topics as features in a Random Forest model to predict six different Likert-scale satisfaction ratings, interpreting feature importance to understand drivers of satisfaction. Third, as a robustness check, it clusters topics into positive and negative groups and uses the prevalence of these clusters as predictors in a two-way fixed-effects regression model, controlling for administrative and demographic variables.

paper_id: Kroeber - 2023 - When Do Men MPs Claim to Represent Women in Plenary Debates—Time-Series Cross-Sectional Evidence fro

metadata.title: When Do Men MPs Claim to Represent Women in Plenary Debates—Time-Series Cross-Sectional Evidence from the German States

data.data_source_detail: full plenary minutes for the six German states (Baden-Wuerttemberg, Brandenburg, Bremen, Hamburg, Rhineland-Palatinate, and Saarland)
data.corpus_size_raw: Of all speeches delivered in the six states over three electoral periods, 7.19% mention the word women at least once.
data.preprocessing_details: I conducted the common steps to prepare a text for automated text analysis including (1) transforming all text to lower cases, (2) removing stop words (in German), (3) removing punctuation and numbers, (4) removing short words with less than four characters, and ( 5) reducing all words to their stem. I furthermore excluded words that are extremely common and do not distinguish single observations or topics (if a word was used in more than half of all speech sets) as well as all words that are used scarcely and hence contain little information about topics (used on average less than one time by every party in the six state parliaments).

model.K_evidence: While the overall model estimated 14 topics, this analysis focuses on six of them which are clearly linked to the hypotheses.

K_selection.selection_evidence: I used the "ldatuning" package (Murzintcev, 2020) to identify the preferable number of topics given the criteria of semantical coherence and exclusiveness (see Figure OA1 in the Online Appendix).

components.epistemic_function_evidence: To test my main propositions, I regress the first differences of the prevalence of the six topics on sex group and the share of women MPs belonging to a party parliamentary group.
components.downstream_description: The study uses the prevalence of six topics as dependent variables in a series of linear panel data regression models. These models test how the change in topic prevalence in speeches by male MPs is associated with the share of women in their party's parliamentary group. The analysis includes visualizations of predicted margins, numerous robustness checks, and subgroup analyses based on party ideology and state-level characteristics.
components.downstream_evidence: To test my main propositions, I regress the first differences of the prevalence of the six topics on sex group and the share of women MPs belonging to a party parliamentary group. The models predict the average change in legislative behavior of all men or respectively all women belonging to a party parliamentary group from 1 year to the next within a legislative term.

downstream.downstream_description: The study uses topic prevalence as a measure of issue attention in legislative speeches. These prevalences are then used as dependent variables in a series of panel data regression models to test hypotheses about how the share of women in a political party influences the topics male MPs discuss. The analysis includes numerous robustness checks and subgroup comparisons.

downstream.downstream_description: The primary downstream analysis involves using 100 topics to construct a two-dimensional 'space of epistemological position taking' via Multiple Correspondence Analysis. components.epistemic_function_evidence: In this section, we provide an illustrative example of how machine translation can be used in conjunction with the STM to make comparisons across countries and languages.

components.epistemic_function_evidence: We want to ensure that the topics are uncovering differences in semantic content rather than linguistic idiosyncrasies in describing that content.

components.downstream_evidence: For purposes of comparison, we ran a topic model that did not include a content covariate. Within this topic model, the word "Snowdon" pinned down its own topic... components.epistemic_function_evidence: Given the dramatic cost difference between the full-text and DTM translations, it is useful to investigate the similarity of the resulting topic models. If the DTM translation produces comparable results, it will clearly be preferable on cost alone.
components.downstream_description: The study compares the outputs of topic models based on two different translation strategies (full-text vs. document-term matrix) to assess the robustness of the findings. components.downstream_evidence: We examine the alignment between models by comparing the topic-word distributions of all topics in both models. downstream.downstream_description: The provided text details two distinct applications of Structural Topic Models (STM). The first analyzes a corpus of religious texts to compare the topics discussed by Jihadist and non-Jihadist clerics, using cleric status as a predictor in the STM. This analysis includes visualizing topic correlations as a network, plotting specific topic proportions against each other to explore intra-group differences, and plotting a derived topic measure against cleric birth year to show temporal trends. The second application compares social media reactions to the Edward Snowden leaks in Chinese and Arabic, using language and time as covariates in the STM. This section also includes methodological demonstrations comparing models with and without a content covariate to handle translation artifacts and comparing results from different upstream translation strategies (full-text vs. DTM).

components.downstream_description: A topic model was used to identify key themes (police brutality, China's influence, protest events) in the tweet corpus. These subsets were then used as inputs for sentiment analysis, regression, auto-regression, and Granger causality tests.
components.downstream_evidence: First, it is important to identify the primary parties involved in the anti-ELAB movement. To do so, the topic modelling technique was employed. Put simply, topic modelling is a type of unsupervised machine learning, meaning that it can be carried out without the active input of predefined labels or categories (the so-called training set). Its goal is to represent each document as a mixture of topics. For this study, the topic modelling technique was employed to identify topic domains which would be useful for further investigation... Drawing upon these results, it could be deduced that English tweets posted from abroad revolved primarily around three topics, namely, police brutality, China's influence as well as various protest events initiated by the protestors. I then used several corresponding keywords to extract these subsets of tweets from the entire dataset (see Table 3). These subsets of tweets then served as the input for simple regression, auto-regression and Granger causality tests as explained above.

downstream.downstream_description: The study uses a topic model to identify the main themes within tweets about the anti-ELAB movement. These themes (police brutality, China's influence, protest events) are then used to generate keywords for filtering the dataset into thematic subsets. These subsets are subsequently analyzed using sentiment analysis and regression models to compare public opinion across different facets of the movement and to test hypotheses regarding the impact of reported violence on sentiment and attention.

downstream.downstream_description: The study uses topic proportions from a structural topic model as the basis for its analysis. The term list was manually processed to merge semantically equivalent words and filter out terms with no semantic meaning. The final matrix was tf-idf weighted.

model.K_evidence: a manual analysis of models from K=28 to K=32 resulted in the selection of K=29 for the final model.

K_selection.selection_evidence: We calculated models using the new feature matrix over a wide range of values for K and evaluated them based upon the same held-out likelihood and residuals metrics from before, but with the constraint that the final K had to represent a plausible number of distinct frames relaxed. Consequently, a value for K closer to the optimum number of topics was identified, c. K=30, and a manual analysis of models from K=28 to K=32 resulted in the selection of K=29 for the final model.

labeling.labeling_evidence: As a final step, we characterized each topic according to its keywords and the documents which load on it most heavily, then had two coders independently identify irrelevant topic clusters and attempt to combine those which remained into coherent frame packages (inspired by Walter & Ophir, 2019, and their combination of large-K STM groupings for frame identification).

components.epistemic_function_evidence: The Australia results look promising, with Topics 1, 2, and 4 most clearly interpretable as being coherent and having a problem definition and causal diagnosis.
components.downstream_description: A standard Structural Topic Model (STM) is run on two different corpora (a narrow Australian dataset and a broader USA Today dataset).
